coredata.dc:title: Construction of NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs nanocomposites for double anti-counterfeiting fluorescence ink
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Qing
Wu, Haoyi
Jin, Yahong
Wang, Chuanlong
Zhang, Ruiting
Hu, Yihua
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent nanomaterials with multiple anti-counterfeiting play a vital role in economic and social fields in our daily lives. In this work, a hybridized composite of boron and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots (B, N-GQDs) and lanthanide-doped up-conversion NaYF4 was prepare for a double anti-counterfeiting application. The composite was obtained by one-pot hydrothermal method and it was commixed with glycerol in a ratio of 4 : 1 to prepare a fluorescence ink, which exhibited independent blue and green fluorescence emissions under ultraviolet light and near-infrared laser excitation. Therefore, it had a considerable application in confidential communications, logo designing and file encryptions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NaYF4: Yb
Ho
B
N-GQDs
Fluorescence ink
Nanocomposites
Double anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of synthesizing NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs and the application of anti-counterfeiting fluorescence ink.
The XRD patterns of B, N-GQDs, NaYF4: Yb, Ho, NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs. and the standard data for β-NaYF4 (JCPDS NO 28–1192) and α-NaYF4(JCPDS No 77–2042).
TEM images (a) for B, N-GQDs, insets are particle size distribution and HRTEM image, (b) for NaYF4: Yb, Ho, (c) and (d) for NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs, inset is HRTEM image.
Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) of the elemental mapping for NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs.
(a) XPS spectra of B, N-GQDs, (b) XPS spectra of NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs and high-resolution peaks of C1s (c), B1s (d), N1s (e), O1s(f).

(a) FT-IR spectra and (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of B, N-GQDs, NaYF4: Yb, Ho, NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs, (c) the excitation-dependent spectrum of NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs and (d) the emission and excitation spectrum of GQDs, B, N-GQDs and NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs.

(a) the up-conversion emission spectrum of NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs, (b) the up-conversion luminescent intensity changed with the different laser power densities and (c) the corresponding laser power dependence of the emission of NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs. (d) energy level diagrams of NaYF4: Yb, Ho.
The optical image of fluorescence ink written on filter paper under visible light, UV lamp (365 nm), infrared laser (980 nm) irradiation.

NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs fluorescence emission intensity at different temperatures (a) for DC emission, (c) for UC emission, emission intensity comparison of room temperature and change temperature (b) for DC emission, (d) for UC emission and (e) for the actual optical image.

head.ce:title: Construction of NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs nanocomposites for double anti-counterfeiting fluorescence ink

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Guangdong University of Technology, 510006, HEMC, Guangzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Guangdong University of Technology, 510006, HEMC, Guangzhou, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent nanomaterials with multiple anti-counterfeiting play a vital role in economic and social fields in our daily lives. In this work, a hybridized composite of boron and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots (B, N-GQDs) and lanthanide-doped up-conversion NaYF4 was prepare for a double anti-counterfeiting application. The composite was obtained by one-pot hydrothermal method and it was commixed with glycerol in a ratio of 4 : 1 to prepare a fluorescence ink, which exhibited independent blue and green fluorescence emissions under ultraviolet light and near-infrared laser excitation. Therefore, it had a considerable application in confidential communications, logo designing and file encryptions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continued growing of global production, anti-counterfeiting has become a big concern for the priority of genuine production. It has been widely used in the fields of currencies, luxuries, foods and medicines [1–4]. By now, scientists and engineers around the world have been working on exploring novelty anti-counterfeiting technology, including the quick response (QR) or barcode codes [5–8], laser sensitive labels [9], temperature and optical sensitive tags [10,11], hidden tags [12,13], radio frequency identification (RFID) tags [14] and so on. However, these strategies need either a network communication or a complex synthesizing process, which is not advantageous to reduce the cost. As compared, identification based on fluorescence is currently a simple and cost-effective method to realize anti-counterfeiting. In this way, the performance of fluorescent materials, including phosphors [15–17], inks [18,19] and adhesive [20] play a crucial role in the anti-counterfeiting results. Among these materials, composite with the ability of double or multiple anti-counterfeiting is favorable for high security.

In order to avoid the signal overlapping, the fluorescent anti-counterfeiting is usually employed different excitation sources, i.e. spontaneous and stimulated (laser) emissions, hence different responses induced for double (or more) anti-counterfeiting are required for a composite material. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have unique physical properties, such as electronic conductivity, photoluminescence and magnetism. Apparently, it is widely used in the fields of bio-sensing and imaging [21,22], drug delivery [23–25], magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) contrast agent [26], ion detection [27–29] and photocatalysis [30]. In recent years, the GQDs were demonstrated feasible using in fluorescent anti-counterfeiting. In order to improve the sensitivity, high fluorescent efficiency is favorable for ensuring the responsible signal. Previously, the B, N, P or S element was attempted to dope into the GQDs to modify the surface or edge functional groups [31–34], thereby improving their fluorescent performance. On the other hand, lanthanides doped up-conversion (UC) material is another promising candidate in anti-counterfeiting since it is only sensible to laser, which is a specific anti-counterfeiting strategy. Therefore, it is plausible to hybridize GQDs and UC material to realize a UV-lamp-infrared (IR)-laser-response double anti-counterfeiting strategy. For example, Zhang et al. [35]. reported a GQDs and Er and Yb co-doped NaYF4 composite with both fluorescence and UC emission for an anti-counterfeiting film. However, the hybridized formation of GQDs and UC material is still a problem needed to be addressed because the decoration of GQDs on bulk UC materials is insufficiently homogeneous, and the nano-size UC may result in the aggregation against the fluorescent efficiency. It is necessary to explore a new approach for better hybridizing the materials and simultaneously preserving an effective luminescent property.

In current work, we tried to use a surfactant to modify a hydrothermal (HT) process and the GQDs were co-doped by B and N for the purpose of improved fluorescence through a charge balance doping condition. We found that the particles composed of GQDs and NaYF4: Yb, Ho were obtained within 100 nm. The particles were dispersible in water and feasible to fabricate a transparent ink that showed blue and green fluorescence under UV and IR laser excitation, respectively. Therefore, this ink was writable or printable on paper with visible patterns in UV or IR laser excitation, showing the potential application in double anti-counterfeiting or other confidential cases.

All chemicals were used of analytical grade and without further purification. B5H4NO8, YN3O9·6H2O and YbN3O9·5H2O were obtained from Macklin Co. Ltd. The glycerol, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), NaF and citric acid were from Zhiyuan Chemical reagent Co. Ltd. The deionized (DI) water used to prepare all aqueous solutions were provided by Guangzhou Chemical Industry.

The B, N-GQDs were synthesized using HT method. Typically, 0.525 g citric acid, 0.5 g ammonium pentaborate and 25 mL DI water were poured into a beaker and stirred for 10 min. The mixture was sealed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. After the reaction, the autoclaved mixture was cooled for 24 h to room temperature. Besides, the synthesis of GQDs is same as B, N-GQDs without adding 0.5 g ammonium pentaborate. The resulting solution was sonicated to gain a light yellow liquid containing B, N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The composite of NaYF4: Yb, Ho and B, N-GQDs (NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs) was synthesized by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method [36]. Firstly, 0.78 mmol YN3O9·6H2O, 0.2 mmol YbN3O9·5H2O, 0.02 mmol HoN3O9·5H2O and 1 mmol EDTA-2Na were dissolved into 14 mL distilled water and stirring for 30 min what's more, 12 mmol NaF and 11 mL distilled water were added into a beaker and both stirred for 30 min to form solution A. In a typical ratio, the molar ratios of lanthanide: ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA-2Na): NaF powder was 1 : 1: 12. Secondly, the above mixture was sealed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and HT treated at 180 °C for 24 h. After cooling for 24 h, the solution containing NaYF4: Yb, Ho was obtained. The NaYF4: Yb, Ho/GQDs was synthesized by adding 2.5 mmol citric acid solution A, kept string for 10 min and the HT procedure was repeated. The synthesis of NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs is the same as NaYF4: Yb, Ho/GQDs, with a difference of adding 2.5 mmol ammonium pentaborate additionally.

For the anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink, 4 mL NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs solution and 1 mL glycerol were added to the beaker under ultrasonic treatment for 5 min to obtain a homogeneous and thick mixture. Afterward, the as-prepared fluorescent ink was loaded into an empty pen tube, and a writable prototype was realized on various paper (i. e. filter paper which is fast drying without fluorescence background).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) was employed to study the crystallization degree of B, N-GQDs and other samples on a diffractometer (D8 ADVANCE). The fluorescent emission and excitation spectra of the obtained materials were measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (FLS-980). Especially, the UC emission spectra of the samples were tested by a FLS-980 fluorescence spectrometer and an external 980 nm IR fiber coupled laser system. The microstructure and crystalline quality of the synthesized samples were observed by a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) from FEI Talos F200S. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) from Thermofisher Nicolet IS50 and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) from Thermo Fisher Escalab 250Xi were employed to analyze the surface functional groups and the elements of the synthesized materials. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were tested by spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU, UV-3600 plus).

A schematically one-pot hydrothermal procedure for the B, N-GQDs, NaYF4: Yb, Ho and their hybridization was illustrated in Fig. 1 , two anti-counterfeiting channels including the introduced with UC NaYF4: Yb, Ho and B, N-GQDs prepared by HT, respectively. Green and blue fluorescence were observed by IR laser and UV lamp excitation, respectively, indicating two independent anti-counterfeiting methods. When the precursor of UC NaYF4: Yb, Ho and B, N-GQDs were commixed and treated using one-pot HT, then the NaYF4: Yb, Ho and B, N-GQDs were hybridized. The hybridization was used to prepare an ink and injected into a pen. A writable anti-counterfeiting pattern could be written on a paper and the pattern exhibited a blue and green emission under UV and IR laser excitation. Therefore, the double anti-counterfeiting prototype was realized using dual fluorescence.

The hybridization of NaYF4: Yb, Ho and B, N-GQDs was not a simple attachment, their formation seemed to involve an embedding process. As shown in Fig. 2 , the crystal structure revealed by XRD patterns indicated that, the graphite-like layers structure reflected by the (001) and (002) diffraction peaks at 14.6 and 27.9° were observed from the B, N-GQDs. The β phase NaYF4 was transformed to α-phase, and the graphite-layers structure from the B, N-GQDs was preserved. The morphology of the B, N-GQDs was around 4 nm, showing a quantum-dot-like feature (Fig. 3 a) and has distinct lattice fringes with 0.24 nm lattice spacing, which is assigned as the (1120) lattice fringes of graphene [37]. On the other hand, the UC NaYF4: Yb, Ho showed an obvious hexagonal structure, indicating the formation of β phase NaYF4. And the morphology of the sample was the rod in bulk with a diameter length about 3 and 10 μm (Fig. 3b). After hybridization, the bulk NaYF4 disappeared and particles with 50 nm were obtained in Fig. 3 (c). According to the XRD patterns and morphology analysis, the NaYF4: Yb, Ho and B, N-GQDs were hybridized successfully and the GQDs were probably embedded into the α-NaYF4. The HRTEM of NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs was exhibited in Figs. 3 (d), 0.31 nm and 0.24 nm lattice fringes were associated with (111) diffraction of α-NaYF4 and (100) of the GQD, respectively. The Raman spectrum of B, N-GQD and NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQD were shown in Fig. S1 and the intensity ratio ID/IG was 0.84 and 1.64, respectively. As we all know, the D band is regarded as disordered carbonaceous structure and the G band is regarded as an order graphitic structure [38]. These results indicate that the presence of edges and isolated defects in B, N-GQD due to born and nitrogen dopants in graphitic lattice planes [39]. Moreover, the process of UC materials hybridization can increase the defect of B, N-GQDs. The AFM image of B, N-GQD was exhibited in Figs. S2 and B, N-GQD have an average height of 1.2 ± 0.4 nm, indicating 1–4 layered structures [40].

In order to confirm this conformation, the EDS with element mapping of the hybridized sample was shown in Fig. 4 . The EDS of the sample revealed that energy from typical C, N, B, Na, Y, F, Yb and Ho dispersion were observed. And the mapping confirmed that all the elements were distributed on the particles homogeneously. It was difficult to distinguish the components of B, N-GQDs and NaYF4: Yb, Ho, indicating the blending of two materials. Hence it was plausible that GQDs were embedded into the α-NaYF4, forming an interwoven network.

To determine the chemical composition of NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs composite, the XPS analysis was carried out and the results were shown in Fig. 5 (b). Several energy peaks at around 1070，684，532，400，285，192，153 eV were observed in the survey spectrum, corresponding to the Na 1s, F 1s, O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, B 1s and Yb 4d, Y 3d electronic energy, confirming the elements from the composite. The deconvolution of the C 1s peak (Fig. 5 c) indicated the existence of C–B, C–C/CC, C–O, C–N–C and O–CO bonds [41] from the B, N-GQDs, and the doping of B and N was further confirmed by the B 1s, and N 1s peaks observed at around 192 and 400 eV. Deconvolution of B 1s and N 1s exhibited the B–C, B–O, pyridine-N, pyrrolic-N, quaternary-N and N–O bonds responsible for the peaks at 191.6, 192.4, 398.8, 399.7, 401.5 and 406.5 eV (Fig. 5 d and e) [42–44], respectively. The two peaks appearing at 531.6 eV and 532.5 eV in the O1s spectrum were attributed to C–OH/C–O–C and CO bonds in Fig. 5 (f). The results confirmed the formation of B and N when they were doped into the GQDs. Besides, the full scan XPS spectrum of B, N-GQDs was shown in Fig. 5 (a) and C1s, B1s, N1s and O1s of B, N-QDs are shown in Fig. S3.

Besides, the FT-IR spectra in Fig. 6 (a) also confirmed the hybridization of the NaYF4: Yb, Ho and B, N-GQDs. The B, N-GQDs sample showed the absorption peaks at 3220, 1708 and 1655 cm−1, corresponding to the N–H stretching, and CO stretching, N–CO stretching vibration, indicating the successful doping of N. The absorption peaks at 1195, 1078, 919 and 776 cm−1 corresponding to the B–O–H bending, C–B stretching, N–B–O stretching, and B–N stretching vibration [41,45,46] indicated the successfully doping of B. For the NaYF4: Yb, Ho sample, two obvious absorption peaks were observed, which corresponded to CO–C stretching vibration at 1618 cm−1 and C–N stretching vibration at 1345 cm−1 [47,48]. These chemical components came from the surfactant during the synthesis. After two materials were hybridization, the feature absorption peaks for the B, N-GQDs were suppressed, resulting from the embedding of the GQDs into the inner part of the NaYF4: Yb, Ho. This embedding also reduced the amount of surfactant whose FT-IR absorption peaks were also reduced.

The optical absorption spectra of the sample were shown in Fig. 6 (b). All samples showed an absorption shoulder below 500 nm, and an obvious absorption peak at about 330 nm was observed from the B, N-GQDs and their composite. This absorption was caused by the n-π* transition. No obvious peak was observed from the NaYF4: Yb, Ho, indicating the band-to-band transition was induced when it was exciting.

The particles of NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs composite was dispersive in water, forming a light yellow solution. As shown in Fig. 6 (c), excitation-dependent spectra of NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs were exhibited. It can be observed that when the excitation wavelengths were gradually increased from 300 nm to 390 nm, the maximum photoluminescence peaks showed a significant red-shift from 410 nm to 480 nm and the maximum excitation and emission wavelength of NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs at 330 nm and 430 nm, respectively. The excitation-dependent photoluminescence phenomenon of NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs due to the different particle sizes and different surface states were formed in the vicinity of the conduction. Besides, the same excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior of GQDs, B, N-GQDs and NaYF4: Yb, Ho/GQDs were found in Fig. S4. In order to investigate the effects of boron and nitrogen doping and composite UC materials on GQD, the maximum excitation and emission wavelength of GQDs, B, N-GQDs and NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs were shown in Fig. 6 (d). These results indicating that dopants and hybrid UC materials can cause a redshift of GQD emission spectrum. More interestingly, dopants and hybrid UC materials can also enhance photoluminescence intensity significantly in Fig. S4 (d)， which may contribute to the rich oxygen-containing groups on the surface of GQD and the UC materials can enhance the energy absorption of GQD in 280–500 nm area. In conclusion, the doping of boron and nitrogen and composite UC materials not only enhanced the luminescent intensity, but also induced a red-shift of the GQDs to the range that was more sensitive to the human eye. Besides, as shown in Figs. S5 and S6, for blue emission, the quantum yield of GQDs is 3.88%, B, N-GQDs is 11.38%, NaYF4: Yb, Ho/GQDs is 7.90% and NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs is 13.19%, indicating that hybrid UC materials and dopants can enhance quantum yield obviously. On the other hand, when the composite was excited by a 980 nm laser, a green emission at 540 nm accompanied by a small peak at 645 nm was observed in Fig. 7 (a). These emissions were ascribed to the 5S2/5F4→5I8 and 5F5→5I8 transition configuration of the Ho3+ ions in NaYF4. A 980 nm laser induced a 540 and 645 emission, confirming the typical UC luminescence [49]. Therefore, two anti-counterfeiting channels were achieved in one composite with UV lamp and IR laser excitation respectively. The luminescence of the composite was optimized by controlling the heating time for HT and the cooling rate, which were 24 h and 5.4 °C/h for the optimized luminescent intensity in Fig. S7 (a) and (b).

In order to confirm the UC in NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs, the laser power density dependence of UC emission intensities were investigated and the result was shown in Fig. 7 (b). For an unsaturated UC process, the monitored UC emission intensity (I) was proportional to Pn, where P is infrared pump power, and n is the number of laser photons required in populating the upper emitting state [50]. As shown in Fig. 7 (c), the n value was figured out of NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs at 540 nm and 645 nm are 1.27 and 1.37, respectively. The results indicated the energy transition between Yb and Ho was a two-photon process. This process can clearly described by energy level diagrams of Yb3+ and Ho3+ in Fig. 7 (d). The Yb3+ ions continuously absorbed the photons of infrared laser (λex = 980 nm), and transferred the energy to Ho3+ ions, which were then excited from 5I8 to 5I6 and then to 5F5 via 5I7 or directly to 5S2/5F4, inducing the emission at 645 and 540 nm, respectively.

In order to realize the encryption for double anti-counterfeiting by writing or printing, the fluorescent ink was prepared using the hybridized NaYF4: Yb, Ho/B, N-GQDs composite and glycerol. After adding into glycerol, the ink fluorescence intensity is reduced by 20% (in Fig. S8 a and b), but the viscosity is greatly increased to facilitate better writing and printing. As shown in Fig. 8 , the fluorescent ink was injected into a pen and a variety of characters including Chinese characters, alphabets and pattern were easily written on a paper. Under visible light excitation, the color of the filter paper has not changed and the written characters were invisible. When the excitation source was changed to UV light (λex = 365 nm), blue fluorescent characters were observed on the paper, corresponding to the luminescence of B, N-GQDs part of the particles. Moreover, when the excitation source was changed to an IR laser (λex = 980 nm), the emission color of the characters was changed to green, corresponding to the luminescence of NaYF4: Yb, Ho parts of the particles. Besides, the stability of the fluorescence of the ink was evaluated in a high temperature environment, DC and UC emission spectra from 30 to 150 °C temperature gradient were exhibited in Fig. 9 (a) and (c). A series of stable fluorescent intensities were maintained from 30 to 120 °C for both B, N-GQDs and NaYF4: Yb, Ho parts. Thus, the fluorescence ink had excellent temperature resistance. Yet, both intensities were reduced by half at 150 °C (Fig. 9 b and d) due to the thermal quenching of the excitation states, indicating an applicable temperature range below this temperature. Thus, optical image prototype was seen by naked eye more intuitively at 30–120 °C, which conformed to the emission intensity.

In summary, the nanocomposite of B, N-GQDs and NaYF4: Yb, Ho was synthesized by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method, showing two independent luminescent colors under ultraviolet light (λex = 365 nm) and near-infrared laser (λex = 980 nm) irradiation. The luminescence was induced by the n-π* electron transition of B, N-GQDs and two-photon process of NaYF4: Yb, Ho. Based this composite, the as-obtained fluorescence ink prepared by mixing the composite with glycerol showed the advantages of nanometer scale, double anti-counterfeiting, high temperature stability and so on. Therefore, it indicated the application prospects not only in confidential communication, but also in advanced printing technology, commodity logo designing and file encryption.

Qing Yao: Sample preparation, performance testing, data analysis, paper writing, Haoyi Wu: Experimental supervision and guidance, paper review and revision, Yahong Ji: Experimental guidance, data analysis, Chuanlong Wang, Ruiting Zhang: Experimental supervision and guidance, data verification, Yihua Hu: Experimental supervision and guidance, paper review.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.51972065) and Guangdong Natural Science Foundation (No. 2021A1515010179). We would like to thank Analysis and Test Center of Guangdong University of Technology for TEM and AFM analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2022.119186.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cooperative supramolecular polymers with anthracene‒endoperoxide photo-switching for fluorescent anti-counterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gao Z, Han Y, Wang F. Cooperative supramolecular polymers with anthracene-endoperoxide photo-switching for fluorescent anti-counterfeiting. Nat. Commun 2018;9:3977.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strategies to detect and reduce counterfeiting activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Berman B. Strategies to detect and reduce counterfeiting activity. Bus. Horizons 2008;51:191-199.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Versatile and validated optical authentication system based on physical unclonable functions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Arppe-Tabbara R, Tabbara M, Soerensen TJ. Versatile and Validated Optical Authentication System Based on Physical Unclonable Functions. ACS Appl Mater Interfaces 2019;11:6475-6482.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stretchable, flexible, and transparent SrAl2O4:Eu2+@TPU ultraviolet stimulated anti-counterfeiting film

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gao W, Ge W, Shi J, Tian Y, Zhu J, Li Y. Stretchable, flexible, and transparent SrAl2O4:Eu2+@TPU ultraviolet stimulated anti-counterfeiting film. Chem. Eng. J 2021;405:126949.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An IoT-based anti-counterfeiting system using visual features on QR code

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yan Y, Zou Z, Xie H, Gao Y, Zheng L. An IoT-Based Anti-Counterfeiting System Using Visual Features on QR Code. IEEE Internet Things J 2021;8:6789-6799.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Drug-laden 3D biodegradable label using QR code for anti-counterfeiting of drugs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Fei J, Liu R. Drug-laden 3D biodegradable label using QR code for anti-counterfeiting of drugs. Mater. Sci. Eng. C 2016;63:657-662.
Zhou Y, Zhao G, Bian J, Tian X, Cheng X, Wang H, et al. Multiplexed SERS Barcodes for Anti-Counterfeiting. ACS Appl Mater Interfaces 2020;12:28532-28538.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Constructing perovskite-like oxide CsCa2Ta3O10: Yb, Er@Cs(PbxMn1-x)(ClyBr1-y)3 perovskite halide composites for five-dimensional anti-counterfeiting barcodes applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wan W, Han X, Zhou Y, Chen F, Jing X, Ye S. Constructing perovskite-like oxide CsCa2Ta3O10: Yb, Er@Cs(PbxMn1-x)(ClyBr1-y)3 perovskite halide composites for five-dimensional anti-counterfeiting barcodes applications. Chem. Eng. J 2021;409:128165.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Upconversion modulation through pulsed laser excitation for anti-counterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Han Y, Li H, Wang Y, Pan Y, Huang L, Song F, et al. Upconversion Modulation through Pulsed Laser Excitation for Anti-counterfeiting. Sci Rep 2017;7:1320.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inverse thermal quenching effect in lanthanide-doped upconversion nanocrystals for anti-counterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lei L, Chen D, Li C, Huang F, Zhang J, Xu S. Inverse thermal quenching effect in lanthanide-doped upconversion nanocrystals for anti-counterfeiting. J Mater Chem C 2018;6:5427-5433.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation and RGB upconversion optic properties of transparent anti-counterfeiting films

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yao W, Tian Q, Liu J, Xue Q, Li M, Liu L, et al. Preparation and RGB upconversion optic properties of transparent anti-counterfeiting films. Nanoscale 2017;9:15982-15989.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Color-switchable hybrid dots/hydroxyethyl cellulose ink for anti-counterfeiting applications
